behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Recursive watch behavior is a real filesystem/OS event-boundary concern. In-process mocks can claim subdirectory events exist even when the actual watcher setup misses them on real platforms.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp input/output directory tree. Create `input/sub/a.txt` after startup. Assert exactly one transformed output appears for that nested file path within timeout. Run on the target OS/filesystem used in production.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: The core risk is wiring between event intake, dedup cache/window, and pipeline dispatch timing. A pure unit test of only the dedup function can miss clock/config/integration mistakes.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon modules together in-process with a real temp filesystem and deterministic clock control (or short real window). Trigger two arrivals for the same path within window; assert one pipeline execution. Trigger again after window; assert second execution occurs.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: This failure mode is specifically OS watcher event multiplicity/coalescing behavior. Mocked watcher events can miss real burst patterns and timing races.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to emit multiple events (e.g., open/write/close or temp-file-rename). Capture pipeline invocation count for that file and assert exactly one processing result.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Watcher invalidation and re-subscription after delete/recreate is OS/filesystem dependent and often fails only with real watch handles.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real directory. Delete the watched directory, recreate it with the same path, then add a new file. Assert the daemon processes the new file without restarting process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real filesystem timestamp semantics (resolution, tie behavior, stat timing) plus event ordering races; mocks can hide those edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files nearly simultaneously in a real watched directory with controlled timestamp differences where possible. Record actual processing order and compare against each file’s creation timestamp order (with explicit tie-break rule if spec defines one).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: If no tie-break rule exists, this test should only assert strict ordering when timestamps differ; otherwise requirement is underspecified.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Partial-write detection depends on real write timing, OS event emission, and file visibility during writes. Mocked dependencies frequently hide premature reads.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on real filesystem. Begin writing a large file in chunks with delays (keep fd open). Verify no processing/output appears until final close/completion signal. After close, assert exactly one successful processing run.
